topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee did little to warm the chill settling deep in my chest. I stared at the resume Sarah had casually mentioned—a title that sounded impressive, a career path I desperately craved. Every time I looked at her, a familiar knot tightened in my stomach, a dull, heavy pressure that made breathing shallow. I’d always felt like an afterthought, a placeholder in everyone else’s narrative. When she spoke about her new role at the design firm, her eyes shone with an easy confidence that felt like a physical slap. It wasn't until I stumbled upon an old project file on her shared drive, a misplaced folder containing internal memos, that the foundation of my quiet despair began to crack. The details didn't align. The jargon was too specific, the project scope completely different from the polished presentation she’d given me. A slow, creeping numbness spread through my limbs, leaving me feeling disconnected, as if I were watching my own life unfold from behind thick, distorting glass.

[story 2]

My hands shook so badly I had to grip the edge of the table until my knuckles were white. I had spent the entire afternoon trying to construct a believable defense against the suspicion blooming inside me. Sarah’s story about her promotion sounded so smooth, so utterly convincing, yet the evidence I’d pieced together—a series of inconsistent dates, a document that looked suspiciously like a template, the way her gaze flickered away whenever I pressed for specifics—was a relentless, suffocating weight. I felt physically sick, a churning nausea rising in my throat. *Why couldn't I see it?* The thought echoed hollowly. I looked down at my own hands, small and clumsy, and felt an overwhelming urge to simply dissolve into the floorboards. The silence in the room amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. I just wanted the pressure to stop, the constant, low-grade ache of inadequacy that had been my constant companion finally demanding release.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating, high-pitched whine, mirroring the frantic buzzing in my skull. I watched Sarah across the breakroom, her laughter bright and unrestrained, a sound that seemed impossibly distant. Every shared glance felt like a deliberate exclusion. I had spent weeks building up the courage to ask her about the details of her new position, hoping for some shared validation, but the moment I tried, the carefully constructed facade she presented crumbled, revealing jagged edges beneath. When I finally confronted her, the words came out as a dry rasp. Her reaction wasn't denial; it was a swift, practiced deflection, a sudden, icy composure that made my own internal turmoil feel pathetic and exposed. I retreated quickly, the sensation of being utterly transparent washing over me. I leaned against the cool wall, closing my eyes, feeling the sheer emptiness expand within my core, a profound sense of being fundamentally misplaced in the world.
